label: Germany — Technical Recession
sub: Europe's largest economy entered technical recession in 2024–2025, hit by energy costs from Russia sanctions, weak Chinese demand for exports, and US tariff exposure. GDP contracted two consecutive quarters; manufacturing PMI persistently in contraction.

label: United Kingdom — Stagnation Risk
sub: UK GDP contracted 0.1% in Q4 2024, narrowly avoiding technical recession. CPI projected to peak at 4% in Sep 2025; Bank Rate cut 6 times to 3.75% since Aug 2024. Consumer confidence weak as cost-of-living squeeze persists.

label: Argentina — 220% Inflation Crisis
sub: Argentina suffered 220% annual inflation in 2024, the highest in the world among major economies. President Milei's 'chainsaw' austerity program slashed government spending and deregulated the economy. GDP contracted sharply before beginning stabilization under IMF program.

label: Turkey — Rate Shock Stabilization
sub: Turkey shifted to monetary orthodoxy in 2023–2024, raising rates dramatically from ~8% to 40%+ to combat chronic inflation. The lira lost significant value against the dollar. The rate shock caused a severe economic slowdown before inflation began to moderate.

label: Sri Lanka — Post-Default IMF Recovery
sub: Sri Lanka defaulted on its sovereign debt in 2022 and began an IMF restructuring program. By 2025, the economy was in fragile recovery mode with significant debt service obligations and tight IMF conditionality constraining growth and social spending.

label: Egypt — IMF Bailout & Currency Crisis
sub: Egypt faced a severe foreign currency crisis in 2024, with the Egyptian pound losing more than half its value against the dollar. Inflation exceeded 30% at peak. The IMF expanded its support program to stabilize the economy. Tourism revenues and Suez Canal income provided some relief.

label: Nigeria — Naira Devaluation Shock
sub: Nigeria devalued the naira in 2023–2024 as part of IMF-backed reforms, leading to sharp inflation above 25% and severe cost-of-living crisis. Oil revenue volatility and fuel subsidy removal compounded economic pressures, contributing to rising recession risk.

label: Pakistan — IMF Bailout & Debt Stress
sub: Pakistan secured a $3B+ IMF Stand-By Arrangement in 2023 and extended program in 2024–2025, averting sovereign default. Foreign reserves had fallen to critical levels. The economy faces high debt-to-GDP, chronic inflation, and structural fiscal deficits requiring continued external support.

label: Washington DC — Federal Reserve & Tariff Epicenter
sub: The Federal Reserve began cutting rates in September 2024 from a 23-year high of 5.50%, delivering 175 bps of cuts to 3.50–3.75% by December 2025. Also the origin of sweeping IEEPA tariffs, subsequently struck down by the Supreme Court in February 2026.

label: Beijing — China Tariff Retaliation Hub
sub: China faced US tariffs of up to 145% in April 2025 before a temporary truce reduced them to ~30%. China retaliated with 84% tariffs on US goods. The PBOC eased monetary policy aggressively to counteract deflationary pressures from the property crisis and trade war.

label: Ottawa — Canada Retaliates Against US Tariffs
sub: Canada was hit with 25% US tariffs in February–March 2025, citing fentanyl and border security. Ottawa announced C$30 billion in retaliatory tariffs on US goods. The Bank of Canada cut interest rates aggressively to cushion the economic blow, particularly to the auto and agricultural sectors.

label: Mexico City — US Tariffs Hit Near-Shoring Boom
sub: Mexico faced 25% US tariffs under IEEPA in early 2025. The country had benefited from 'near-shoring' trends as companies relocated supply chains from China, but tariff threats created new uncertainty. The peso experienced sharp volatility and Pemex's fiscal burden complicated the response.

label: Brussels — EU-US Tariff Negotiations
sub: The EU faced 20% tariffs under Trump's Liberation Day reciprocal tariff scheme (paused for 90 days). European Commission and ECB officials warned of severe growth impacts, particularly for German auto exports. The ECB cut rates eight times to 2.00% partly in response to trade-war headwinds.

label: Frankfurt — ECB's Most Aggressive Easing Cycle
sub: The European Central Bank cut its deposit facility rate eight consecutive times from 4.00% (June 2024) to 2.00% (June 2025) — a 200 bps reduction in 12 months, the most aggressive sustained easing since the eurozone debt crisis. The ECB signaled a pause near neutral rates as eurozone growth showed tentative stabilization.

label: London — Bank of England Rate Cuts
sub: The Bank of England cut its policy rate six times since August 2024, reducing it from 5.25% to 3.75% by early 2026. The MPC navigated the difficult trade-off of weak UK growth against above-target inflation (CPI above 2% target). Governor Andrew Bailey emphasized a gradual approach.

label: Tokyo — BOJ Normalization & Carry Trade Unwind
sub: The Bank of Japan ended negative interest rate policy in March 2024 after 8 years. A July 2024 hike triggered a global yen carry-trade unwind, sending the Nikkei down 12% in August 2024. Japan's 10Y JGB yield rose to 2.11% by February 2026, altering global capital flows.

label: Vietnam — Key Manufacturing Hub Hit by Reciprocal Tariffs
sub: Vietnam was one of the highest-tariffed countries under Trump's Liberation Day tariffs (46% reciprocal rate), targeting its large electronics and textile export sector. As a key alternative manufacturing hub to China, tariff disruption threatened to reverse near-shoring trends that had been driving Vietnam's rapid growth.

label: Seoul — Political Crisis Compounds Trade Risk
sub: South Korea faced both political crisis (President Yoon's martial law attempt, impeachment, and arrest in December 2024) and US tariff risk on its semiconductor and auto exports. The Bank of Korea cut rates to buffer the economic impact, while political instability added investor uncertainty.

label: Rome — Italy BTP-Bund Spread Under Watch
sub: Italy's BTP-Bund yield spread remained a key eurozone stress indicator. With debt-to-GDP near 140%, Italy is dependent on the ECB's Transmission Protection Instrument (TPI) backstop and low borrowing costs. Any ECB policy miscalibration or political crisis could reignite Italian sovereign debt stress.

label: Paris — France Fiscal Deficit Above EU Limits
sub: France's fiscal deficit exceeded 6% of GDP in 2024–2025, well above the EU's 3% ceiling, prompting EU excessive deficit procedure. Political instability following snap elections weakened the government's ability to enact consolidation. French bond spreads widened against German Bunds as fiscal concerns mounted.

label: Shenzhen/Pearl River Delta — China Property Crisis Epicenter
sub: Southern China's property and finance hub, home to Evergrande's headquarters and major developer defaults. The collapse of property giant Evergrande (liquidation ordered January 2024) and Country Garden's default exposed trillions in local government financing vehicle (LGFV) debt. The property sector had accounted for ~25% of China's GDP.

label: Brazil — High Real Interest Rates & Debt Costs
sub: Brazil maintained the Selic rate among the world's highest real interest rates in 2024–2025. High domestic borrowing costs increased debt service burden on the government. Brazil's fiscal deficit expansion fueled concerns about debt sustainability and limited the central bank's room to cut rates to support growth.

label: Sub-Saharan Africa — Debt Refinancing Stress
sub: Multiple Sub-Saharan African economies — including Ghana, Zambia, Ethiopia, and Kenya — faced acute sovereign debt stress in 2024–2025 as dollar debt matured at high interest rates. Several countries sought IMF support programs. Zambia completed an historic restructuring in 2023, setting a precedent for others.

label: New Delhi — India: Tariff Risk vs China Decoupling Benefit
sub: India benefited from US-China decoupling as manufacturing shifted toward India (Apple iPhone production, etc.), but faces its own tariff exposure under Trump's reciprocal tariff scheme. South Asia growth projected at 6.2% for 2026 per World Bank. India's relatively closed economy offers some buffer against global trade slowdown.

label: Beirut — Bank Deposits Frozen, Reconstruction Debt
sub: Lebanon's financial system remained in severe crisis through 2025–2026, with bank deposits frozen since 2019 and no viable restructuring agreement. Post-war reconstruction financing (following 2024 conflict) added to an already unsustainable debt load. The IMF and World Bank were engaged but a reform program remained elusive.

label: Zambia — Post-Default Debt Restructuring Precedent
sub: Zambia completed its landmark sovereign debt restructuring in 2023 — the first African country to restructure under the G20 Common Framework. The successful completion was closely watched as a template for other distressed African debtors (Ethiopia, Ghana) while Zambia itself faces a fragile stabilization period through 2025–2026.

label: US Midwest — Manufacturing Contraction Zone
sub: US manufacturing PMI remained below the 50 expansion/contraction threshold for much of 2024–2025, reflecting both pre-tariff inventory distortions and underlying demand weakness. The Midwest's industrial heartland (autos, steel, heavy equipment) was caught between tariff-induced cost increases and weakened export demand.

label: Shanghai — China Deflation Risk & Export Hub
sub: China's major financial and export hub where deflationary pressures were acutely felt. Chinese CPI hovered near zero throughout 2024–2025 as the property crisis destroyed household wealth and suppressed consumption. Exports were rerouted after US tariffs but at lower prices, feeding global deflationary pressure on goods.

label: Singapore — Asian Trade Bellwether Under Pressure
sub: Singapore's open economy serves as a real-time indicator of Asia-Pacific trade flows. The city-state's electronics exports and container throughput declined materially in tariff-disruption periods. Singapore's central bank (MAS) maintained exchange rate-based policy accommodation to support exports during the global trade war.

label: Amsterdam/Rotterdam — European Trade Gateway at Risk
sub: The Port of Rotterdam, Europe's largest, serves as a key gateway for US and Asian goods to Europe. Under the Liberation Day tariff regime, major re-routing of trade flows was anticipated. The Netherlands' highly open export-dependent economy made it among the most exposed European economies to US tariff disruption.

label: Washington DC — Supreme Court IEEPA Ruling
sub: On February 20, 2026, the US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that IEEPA does not authorize the executive to impose tariffs, striking down most of the 2025 tariff regime. Markets rallied. The administration responded four days later by invoking Section 122 of the Trade Act to reimpose a 10% tariff on $1.2 trillion of imports for 150 days.
